1.evidence: Correctly applied the 1-task-1-verb rule to the transfer scenario and backed it with a concrete, personal practice example (spaghetti noodles dropped from grocery list) showing real friction.

2.evidence: Correctly applied Role-before-task to the transfer scenario and backed it with a concrete before-and-after practice example — specific form warning about squats, plus the surprise that role-first made the model ask about equipment.

weak_spots: did not practice few-shot examples — needs hands-on attempt with own material

1.attempted: used 1-task-1-verb on a meal-prep prompt that asked an assistant to 'plan meals for the week and also make a grocery list and estimate the cost'
1.concrete_detail: turned it into three numbered single-verb steps; the cost estimate only came out right once it was step 3 depending on step 2's list
1.snippet: the model kept giving me meals but silently skipping the cost line until I split it out
1.friction: I first tried two separate prompts instead of one numbered list and lost the shared context between the meals and the list
1.surprise: ordering mattered more than I expected — cost had to come after the grocery list or it just guessed a round number
1.outcome: one numbered list with cost last gave a grocery list that actually added up

2.attempted: applied Role-before-task to a prompt asking for a 4-week beginner workout plan
2.concrete_detail: prepended 'You are a certified strength coach writing for someone who has never lifted' before the task sentence
2.snippet: with the role at the end I got a generic 'do 3 sets of 10'; with it in front it started warning about form and progression
2.friction: I first wrote the task and tacked the coach role on at the end — the plan stayed generic until I moved the role up top
2.surprise: the front-loaded role made it ask about my equipment before planning, which the end-role version never did
2.outcome: role-first turned a boilerplate plan into one scoped to a total beginner

4.attempted: practised Chain-from-data on a prompt asking why my houseplant is dying
4.concrete_detail: made it enumerate the observed symptoms (yellow leaves, soggy soil, low light) as step 0 before guessing a cause
4.snippet: unprompted it jumped straight to 'it needs more water' — the opposite of the soggy-soil symptom
4.friction: I had to explicitly forbid a diagnosis before the symptom list or it guessed immediately
4.surprise: forcing symptoms first flipped the answer from 'underwatered' to 'overwatered'
4.outcome: fact-first ordering stopped the jumped-to-conclusion wrong diagnosis

5.attempted: built a prompt to extract book info into JSON using Template-before-instruction
5.concrete_detail: put the skeleton {"title": "str", "author": "str", "year": "int", "rating": "float"} first, the field explanations after
5.snippet: when I described the fields first it returned a prose paragraph 'The book is...' instead of JSON
5.friction: forgot the inline type hint on 'year' the first time and got the year back as a quoted string
5.surprise: putting the type hint right in the skeleton fixed the quoting without any extra instruction
5.outcome: skeleton-first gave clean parseable JSON every time

7.attempted: practised Diagnose-then-version on a prompt generating product descriptions that came out overly salesy
7.concrete_detail: diagnosis: v1 had no tone constraint and literally said 'make it exciting'; v2 removed 'exciting' and added 'neutral, factual tone, <=2 sentences', kept v1 beside it
7.snippet: v1 kept producing 'You'll LOVE this amazing must-have!' — the word 'exciting' was the culprit
7.friction: my instinct was to rewrite the whole prompt; the diagnosis forced me to change only the tone line
7.surprise: just deleting 'exciting' did most of the work — I'd assumed I needed a whole new prompt
7.outcome: the v1/v2 diff made it obvious the tone word, not the structure, was the problem

8.attempted: used Context-in-brackets to inject a D&D character sheet into a game-master prompt
8.concrete_detail: put the sheet in [CONTEXT: player character, reference only, do not narrate as fact] ... [/CONTEXT] between the GM role and the scene task
8.snippet: without the 'reference only' note it narrated my character's secret backstory out loud to the whole party
8.friction: first attempt interleaved the stats with the instructions and it confused an HP value with a dice roll
8.surprise: the reliability note ('reference only') did more than the delimiters themselves
8.outcome: the labeled block kept the sheet as background instead of script

10.attempted: built a meta-prompt (Prompt-generating-prompts) that generates code-review prompts per language
10.concrete_detail: inputs (language, focus_area); the output is a complete review prompt, not a description of one
10.snippet: v1 returned 'Here is a prompt you could use: ...' wrapper text instead of just the prompt
10.friction: kept getting meta-commentary around the prompt; had to instruct 'output only the prompt, nothing else'
10.surprise: the generated Python-review prompt itself violated 3-negatives-max until I fed that earlier rule back in
10.outcome: chaining the earlier course rules into the meta-prompt made its output pass them
